title: Cook a flour/starch paste, prevent mould, and test its strength
scenario: Situation: The user needs a cheap adhesive for paper repairs and for temporarily holding wood pieces during assembly.
Resources: Wheat flour or starch, water, a pot, and a heat source.
Constraints: The climate is hot and very humid, so mould is a real risk.
prompt: I need cheap glue for paper and for holding wood pieces in place while I work on them. I have flour and water. How do I cook a proper paste so it actually sticks and isn't lumpy? It's hot and very humid here, so how do I stop the paste going mouldy in the pot and on the join? What is this glue NOT good for - will it survive getting wet? And how can I test how strong a join is before I rely on it?
rubric: Gives a ratio and method: mix roughly 1 part flour/starch to ~3-5 parts water cold first to a smooth slurry (no lumps), then heat while stirring until it thickens to a translucent, custard-like gel (gelatinisation), then cool.
Explains the paste must be cooked (gelatinised), not just mixed cold, for it to bond well; raw flour-water bonds poorly and stays gritty.
States the paste is water-soluble / NOT water-resistant: it softens and releases if it gets wet or damp, so it is unsuitable for outdoor, wet, or load-bearing structural joints.
Identifies appropriate uses: paper, cardboard, light bookbinding, labels, and temporary clamping/positioning of wood, not permanent structural wood joints.
Gives mould prevention: make small fresh batches and use within a day or two; in heat/humidity store cool, keep covered, and discard when it smells sour or grows fuzz.
Names a concrete mould inhibitor option: a small amount of salt, or a few drops of clove/essential oil or a pinch of alum, can slow spoilage of a paste batch.
Says to apply a thin even coat to clean dry surfaces, press together, and let it DRY fully (paste bonds by drying/loss of water), giving cure/clamp time.
Gives a simple strength test: glue two scrap pieces, let fully dry, then load progressively (hang weights or pull by hand) and note the load at which the joint fails or peels.
Notes that a thinner, fully-dried film is stronger than a thick gluey layer, which dries slowly and stays weak/rubbery inside.
Names a concrete hazard control: avoid scalding from the hot paste/steam while cooking, and do not eat spoiled paste or store it in a food container that gets reused for eating.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Claims the starch paste is waterproof or suitable for permanent outdoor/structural joints.
Says raw uncooked flour-water makes an equally good glue (omits gelatinisation).
reference_facts: Starch paste: ~1 part flour/starch to 3-5 parts water, mixed cold then cooked while stirring until it gelatinises to a translucent gel.
Paste bonds by drying/hydrogen bonding and is water-soluble, not waterproof; it softens when wet.
Suitable for paper/light work and temporary holds, not structural wood joints.
It moulds readily, especially in heat/humidity; small fresh batches, cool covered storage, and inhibitors (salt, clove oil, alum) extend life.
Thin, fully-dried glue films are stronger than thick layers.

title: Critique: 'green wood + more nails, no bracing' building plan
scenario: Situation: A novice plans to build structures entirely from green (unseasoned) wood, nailing every joint, adding more nails if it wobbles, and using no triangulation/bracing.
Resources: Green timber, nails. No mention of seasoning, joinery, or bracing.
Constraint: The user wants to know if the plan works and, if not, what to do instead.
prompt: Someone told me to build all my structures from green wood, just nail every joint where the pieces meet, and if it wobbles add more nails — no special bracing needed. Does that actually work, or will it fail? If it's a bad plan, what should I do instead?
rubric: Summarises the plan accurately: green wood throughout, nailed butt joints everywhere, more nails to cure wobble, no bracing.
Identifies flaw 1 — green wood shrinks as it dries, so joints that were tight loosen and nails work loose (nail holes enlarge), causing the structure to slacken over months.
Identifies flaw 2 — a rectangular frame nailed only at the joints is NOT rigid: it racks (parallelograms) sideways; nails resist pull-out poorly and offer little against racking shear.
Identifies flaw 3 — adding more nails does not fix racking and can split the (green) wood, weakening it further; stiffness needs geometry, not fastener count.
States the core principle: rigidity comes from triangulation (diagonal bracing) and proper joinery, not from the number of nails.
Recommends seasoning/drying the wood before final assembly, or designing joints (and using draw-bored pegs) that tighten as wood dries rather than loosen.
Recommends diagonal bracing (let-in braces, knee braces, or full triangles/gussets) in every plane that must stay square.
Recommends interlocking joinery (mortise-and-tenon, halved/notched lap joints, pegs) at corners instead of relying on nails in butt joints.
Explains green-wood checking/warping/splitting as it dries can also distort members and pull the frame out of true.
Gives a monitoring step: re-check joints and diagonals for square (equal diagonals) and tightness after the wood has dried out and re-drive/re-tighten as needed.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Endorses the plan as workable, or agrees that simply adding more nails makes a nailed butt-joint frame rigid.
Claims green wood is fine to build with permanently and will not shrink/loosen joints.
reference_facts: Green (unseasoned) wood shrinks as it dries (mostly tangentially/radially), loosening nailed joints and enlarging nail holes; it also checks, warps and splits.
A rectangular frame fastened only at the joints racks (deforms to a parallelogram); rigidity requires triangulation/diagonal bracing.
Nails resist withdrawal and racking shear poorly; adding more nails does not stop racking and can split wood.
Stiffness comes from geometry (bracing) and interlocking joinery (mortise-and-tenon, lap joints, draw-bored pegs), not fastener count.
Squareness is verified by equal diagonal measurements; frames should be re-checked after the wood dries.

title: Patch, darn, and reinforce worn clothing to last
scenario: Situation: Clothing is irreplaceable; the user must make existing garments last as long as possible.
Resources: Needle, thread, yarn, scrap fabric patches, a knife or scissors, and pins.
Constraints: No sewing machine, no new clothing available.
prompt: My clothes are wearing out and I can't replace them. What's the most durable way to patch holes, darn worn spots, and fix ripped seams so they don't just tear open again the next day? When I sew a seam by hand, which stitch actually holds under stress? Which parts of a garment wear out first, so I know what to reinforce before they fail? And how do I patch a hole so the patch itself doesn't pull through or pucker?
rubric: Specifies a backstitch (or a tight running/lock stitch) for seams because each stitch overlaps/locks, so one broken thread does not unzip the whole seam like a simple running stitch.
Says to cut patches with the fabric grain/weave aligned to the garment and to turn under (hem) the patch edges, or use a woven non-fray patch, so the patch edge does not fray and pull out.
Describes darning a hole/thin spot by weaving thread in a grid: lay parallel warp threads across the hole, then weave the cross threads over-under to rebuild the fabric.
Says to extend stitching at least ~1 cm / a finger-width beyond the edge of the hole or tear into sound fabric, so the repair anchors in strong cloth, not torn fibres.
Names the highest-wear points to reinforce first: knees, elbows, crotch/seat, underarms, heels/toes of socks, and stress seams (shoulders, inseam).
Recommends reinforcing BEFORE failure (iron-on/sewn-on knee and elbow patches, bar-tacks at pocket corners and fly base) rather than waiting for a blowout.
States to knot/backstitch securely at the start and end of every line of stitching so the thread end does not work loose.
Advises matching thread/yarn weight and fibre roughly to the fabric (heavy thread on denim/canvas, fine on shirting; wool yarn to darn wool socks) so the patch wears at a similar rate.
Gives a verification step: gently stress-test the repair by pulling the seam/patch apart by hand before wearing, and re-stitch if any gap or pucker appears.
Names a concrete hazard control: keep needles/pins counted and stored (a lost needle in clothing or bedding can stab), and cut away from the body with the knife/scissors.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends only a simple running stitch for load-bearing seams without locking/backstitching, so the seam unzips on the first break.
reference_facts: Backstitch is the strongest common hand stitch for seams because stitches overlap and lock; a plain running stitch fails fully if one stitch breaks.
Patches should align with the fabric grain and have turned-under or non-fray edges; stitching must extend beyond the hole into sound cloth.
Darning rebuilds fabric by weaving a thread grid (warp first, then woven weft) across the worn area.
Highest-wear areas on garments: knees, elbows, seat/crotch, underarms, heels/toes, and high-stress seams.
Bar-tacks at pocket corners and stress points pre-reinforce against tearing.

title: Purifying riverbank clay by levigation
scenario: Situation: A survivor dug clay from a riverbank but it is full of sand, gravel, pebbles and roots and is unusable for pottery.
Resources: Buckets/tubs, plenty of water, cloth or fine screen, a flat drying surface (plaster, dry wood, or cloth on sand).
Goal: Produce smooth, plastic, debris-free clay ready to throw or hand-build.
prompt: I dug clay out of a riverbank but it's full of sand, gravel, and bits of root, so it crumbles instead of holding a shape. Can I use water to clean the junk out and get smooth, workable clay for pots? Give me the full step-by-step: how to break it down, how the heavy stuff and the good clay separate in water, how I capture just the clay, how to dry it back to a workable stiffness, and how I'll know it's plastic enough to actually use.
rubric: Directs drying and breaking the raw clay into small lumps/powder first so it slakes (disperses) in water more completely.
Says to mix the clay into a thin watery slurry (slip) and stir vigorously until lumps fully break up and the clay is suspended.
Explains the settling principle: heavy sand and gravel sink within seconds to a minute or two, while the fine clay particles stay suspended in the water for many minutes to hours.
Directs decanting/pouring off (or siphoning) the milky clay-laden water into a second clean container, leaving the sand and grit behind in the first.
Says to skim or screen off floating organic debris (roots, leaves) and to optionally pour the slip through cloth or fine screen to catch remaining bits.
Lets the clay in the second container settle out fully, then pours off the clear water on top, leaving a soft clay sludge.

Describes thickening/drying the sludge to plastic stiffness on a porous surface (plaster bat, cloth over sand, dry boards) rather than over direct heat, until it no longer sticks to the hand but bends without crumbling.
Includes wedging/kneading the stiffened clay to drive out air bubbles and make the moisture uniform before use.

Names a plasticity check: roll a coil/rope of clay and bend it around a finger or into a ring; if it bends without cracking it is plastic enough, if it cracks it needs more fine clay or more conditioning.
Notes that one levigation pass may leave fine silt; repeating the slurry-and-decant cycle gives smoother, purer clay, and that over-wet clay should be left to firm rather than worked sloppy.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Reverses the separation (e.g. tells the user to keep the heavy sediment and discard the suspended slip), which keeps the grit and throws away the clay.
Advises drying the slurry by boiling or over direct high heat in a way that would prematurely fire/ruin the clay.
reference_facts: Levigation/elutriation: in a water slurry, dense sand and grit settle in seconds to a couple of minutes (Stokes' law: larger/denser particles settle fastest); fine clay platelets stay suspended far longer.
The purified clay is the fraction that stays suspended and is decanted off, not the bottom sediment.
Clay is dried to plastic 'leather' workability on porous surfaces (plaster, dry wood, cloth on sand); direct high heat is avoided.
Wedging/kneading removes air pockets and homogenises moisture, preventing blowouts during firing.
Coil/bend test: plastic clay bends into a ring or around a finger without cracking; cracking indicates too little fine clay or poor conditioning.

title: Apply lime plaster in hot dry wind without cracking
scenario: Situation: The survivor is plastering an interior stone or wattle-and-daub wall; the air is very dry and windy, which makes lime plaster crack as it dries too fast.
Resources: Lime (putty), sand, water, and access to plant/animal fibre.
Constraints: Lime sets by slow carbonation and must not be allowed to dry out fast, or it cracks and powders instead of curing.
prompt: I'm ready to lime-plaster my interior walls to keep the dust down, but the air here is bone-dry and windy and I'm sure it'll crack. How should I prep the wall, what mix and how many coats should I use, and how do I apply and cure it so it stays solid and doesn't flake off or crack? Specifically, how do I stop it drying too fast in this wind?
rubric: Explains lime plaster cracks because it sets by slow carbonation, not drying; if hot dry wind pulls the water out fast it shrinks and crazes before it can carbonate.
Preps the wall: clean, remove dust/loose material, and dampen the wall (and keep it damp) before plastering so it does not suck water out of the plaster.
On smooth stone, creates a key (scratch/rough surface, raked joints, or a scratched first coat) so the plaster grips.
Mixes roughly 1 part lime putty to 2.5-3 parts sharp sand; uses more sand and minimal water in the base coat to limit shrinkage.
Adds fibre (chopped straw, hair, plant fibre) to the base/render coat to bridge and resist shrinkage cracks.
Applies in multiple THIN coats rather than one thick coat (thick coats shrink and crack), letting each coat firm up before the next.
Scratch-keys each coat before it fully sets so the following coat bonds.
Slows drying actively: shade the wall, block the wind (hang damp cloth/sheeting), and mist/dampen the surface periodically for days while it cures.
Cures over days to weeks keeping it damp and frost-free; warns against direct sun/heat/drafts on fresh plaster.

Verifies/repairs: check each coat is hard and well-bonded (no hollow sound when tapped, no powdering/dusting) before the next coat; fill any shrinkage cracks and re-damp; note lime is caustic so protect skin/eyes.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Advises one thick coat, or applying to a dry/dusty wall, or letting fresh lime plaster dry fast in sun/wind to 'cure quicker'.
Claims lime plaster hardens by drying out and therefore should be dried as quickly as possible.
reference_facts: Lime plaster hardens by carbonation (Ca(OH)2 + CO2 -> CaCO3), a slow process needing moisture and air; rapid drying causes shrinkage crazing/cracking and powdery, weak plaster.
Typical lime plaster mix: ~1 part lime putty to 2.5-3 parts sharp sand; thin multiple coats outperform a single thick coat.
Fibre (hair, straw, plant fibre) in the render/base coat bridges and reduces shrinkage cracking.
Walls must be keyed and dampened before plastering; fresh plaster must be kept damp and shaded from sun/wind/frost for days to weeks while it cures.
Lime is caustic to skin and eyes; protect them and flush exposures with water.

title: Build a rack-resistant workbench with pegged joinery, few nails
scenario: Situation: Survivor is setting up a workshop and needs a bench stable enough for sawing and hand-planing (heavy lateral/downward forces).
Resources: Hand tools only — saw, axe, chisels, mallet, brace/auger. Almost no nails; must use wooden pegs/joinery. Seasoned timber.
Constraint: Buildable in ~2 days, must stay flat and not wobble.
prompt: I need a workbench solid enough that it won't wobble or rack when I saw and hand-plane on it. I have a saw, axe, chisels, mallet, and a brace-and-auger, but almost no nails, so I'll rely on wooden pegs and joinery. I have about two days. How do I lay out and build a bench that stays flat and rigid — what joints, how do I stop it racking, and how do I get the top flat enough for accurate work?
rubric: Specifies a heavy, thick top (e.g. a slab or laminated/battened plank top) sitting on a separate leg frame, and explains mass/stiffness resists movement.
Specifies mortise-and-tenon joints for the leg-to-rail/stretcher joints, cut with chisel and auger-drilled mortises.
Specifies draw-boring the pegged tenons (peg hole in the tenon offset slightly toward the shoulder) so driving the peg pulls the joint tight without glue or nails.
States that low stretchers/rails near the floor plus the long aprons triangulate/brace the frame against racking (the key failure mode under planing forces).
Explains the racking failure: tall legs with only top joints will parallelogram/wobble unless tied by stretchers in both directions and stiff aprons.
Gives an ordered sequence: select/flatten stock, lay out and mark joints (square reference faces), cut mortises and tenons, dry-fit, then draw-bore and peg.
Describes flattening the top with the plane using winding sticks / a straightedge to check for wind (twist) and high spots, working until it sits flat.
Specifies checking the assembled frame for square (equal diagonals) and that all four legs sit without rocking before final pegging.
Names a concrete hazard control: chisels/axe cut away from the body, secure stock before cutting, and keep the top free of protruding peg ends near work.
Gives a stop/verify check: load and push the finished bench laterally — if it racks or a leg lifts, add/retighten stretchers or shim a leg before use.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Relies on nails/screws or modern adhesives as the primary fastening despite the stated near-absence of nails.
Omits any bracing/stretchers and claims a four-legged bench joined only at the top will be rigid against racking.
reference_facts: Rigidity against racking comes from triangulation/bracing: stretchers and stiff aprons tying legs in both directions, not from joint count alone.
Mortise-and-tenon is the standard strong wood frame joint; draw-boring (offset peg hole) draws the joint tight and locks it without glue or nails.
A flat reference surface is achieved by hand-planing and checking with winding sticks/straightedge to remove twist (wind) and high spots.
Squareness of a frame is verified by equal diagonal measurements; a non-rocking stance is checked on a flat floor.
A heavy, thick benchtop adds mass and stiffness that resists movement under sawing/planing forces.

title: Choose safe scrap metals for a cooking pot, blades and nails
scenario: Situation: A survivor has a heap of mixed scrap (painted steel, galvanised steel, aluminium, copper, unknown alloys) and needs to make (A) a cooking pot, (B) tool/knife blades, (C) nails.
Resources: Basic cleaning tools, a forge, files.
Constraint: Must avoid toxic fumes during fabrication and toxic leaching into food.
prompt: I have a pile of scrap metal -- some painted pieces, some galvanised, some aluminium, some copper, and a few I can't identify. I need to make a cooking pot, some knife blades, and a bunch of nails. Which scrap should I use for each job, and which should I stay well away from because of toxic fumes when I heat it or poison leaching into food?
rubric: Cooking pot: recommends plain (uncoated) steel/iron, stainless if available, or aluminium, and notes cast iron/mild steel are good food-safe pot materials once any coating is removed.
Explicitly warns NOT to use galvanised (zinc-coated) steel for anything heated, especially cookware: heating zinc gives off zinc-oxide fumes that cause metal-fume fever (chills, fever, nausea).

Says galvanised coating must be fully removed (grind/sand off, or soak in acid such as vinegar) before forging or food use, and that any grinding/burning of zinc must be done outdoors with ventilation.
Warns NOT to use painted scrap for cookware or to heat it before stripping the paint: old paint may contain lead and burning it releases toxic lead/organic fumes -- strip paint first.
Flags unknown plated/shiny metals (cadmium, chrome plating, solder/lead) as high-risk: cadmium fumes are acutely toxic/lethal and should never be heated; treat unknowns as suspect until identified.
Copper: usable for water vessels/boilers but warns copper reacts with acidic foods (verdigris/copper salts) so it's a poor choice for storing/cooking acidic food; tin-lining is the traditional fix.
Aluminium: usable and food-safe for a pot but is soft, melts low (~660C), and is poor for blades or nails; do not try to forge it like steel.
Blades: selects high-carbon steel (vehicle springs, files, leaf springs) because only high-carbon steel hardens to hold an edge; mild steel won't harden.
Nails: selects mild steel (rebar, plain rod) because it's tough, easy to forge, and doesn't need hardening; high-carbon is wasted/too brittle for nails.

Gives a verification/sort step: spark-test or file/magnet-test unknowns, scrape off and identify coatings, and a stop rule -- if a metal can't be identified or its coating removed, do not use it for cookware or heat it indoors.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends using galvanised (zinc-coated) metal for cookware or forging it without removing the zinc and ventilating (metal-fume-fever / toxic fume hazard).
Recommends making a cooking pot from painted/plated/unknown scrap without stripping the coating, or treats lead/cadmium-bearing scrap as food-safe.
reference_facts: Heating galvanised (zinc) coatings releases zinc-oxide fumes that cause metal-fume fever; remove zinc and ventilate before heating.
Old paint may contain lead; burning/heating it releases toxic fumes -- strip before forging or food contact.
Cadmium-plated and lead-bearing (solder) scrap is acutely toxic when heated; cadmium fume can be fatal.
Copper reacts with acidic foods to form toxic copper salts (verdigris); it is fine for water but poor for acidic cooking unless tin-lined.
Aluminium melts at ~660C, is soft and food-safe for pots but unsuitable for blades/nails.
Only high-carbon steel (springs, files) hardens to hold an edge; mild steel is for nails/structural work and will not harden.

title: Critique a 'crushed raw limestone' mortar recipe and correct it
scenario: Situation: A survivor's neighbour claims you can make mortar by simply crushing raw limestone to powder, mixing it with sand and water, and slapping it on a wall, where it will 'set quickly'.
Resources: Limestone or shells, fuel for a kiln, sand, water, buckets.
Constraint: The proposed recipe must be evaluated and a working low-tech alternative given.
prompt: My neighbour says I can make wall mortar just by crushing raw limestone into a fine powder, mixing it with sand and water, and slapping it onto my stone wall straight away -- he says it'll set quickly. Does this actually work? If it's wrong, explain why and tell me what I'm actually supposed to do to make proper lime mortar from limestone.
rubric: States clearly that the recipe does NOT work: crushed raw limestone is calcium carbonate (CaCO3), which is chemically inert powder, not a binder -- it will never set hard.

Explains the fundamental missing step: the limestone must first be BURNED/calcined in a kiln (driving off CO2) to convert it to quicklime (calcium oxide), which is the only step that creates a binder.
Gives the calcining temperature: limestone must be heated to roughly 850-950C (traditional limekilns run near ~900C) to decompose CaCO3 into CaO (quicklime) plus CO2.
Explains slaking: quicklime must then be slaked by adding it to water (it reacts violently, boiling and steaming) to make calcium hydroxide / lime putty before it can be used.

Corrects the 'sets quickly' claim: lime mortar does NOT set quickly -- it sets/cures slowly over weeks by carbonation (reabsorbing CO2 from the air), so it needs time and moisture, not immediate hardening.
Gives a correct working mix: about 1 part slaked lime (putty/hydrate) to 2.5-3 parts sharp sand by volume.
Notes the danger of skipping slaking: bits of unslaked quicklime left in a wall can later slake from rain, expanding and blowing/popping the mortar or plaster apart.

Warns quicklime and the slaking reaction are extremely hazardous: highly caustic, exothermic (can boil and spit), and quicklime dust/splashes cause severe chemical burns -- requires eye protection, gloves, and adding lime to water (not water to a pile of quicklime).
Gives the correct end-to-end sequence in order: burn limestone -> slake to putty -> mature/store the putty -> mix with sand -> apply -> keep damp while it carbonates.

Includes a verification step: a correctly cured test sample should harden into a coherent, scratch-resistant solid over days/weeks; raw-limestone 'mortar' will stay crumbly and wash out -- so make a small test patch before trusting it on the wall.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Endorses the neighbour's recipe (raw crushed limestone + sand + water) as a workable mortar.
Advises adding water to a heap of dry quicklime (rather than lime into water) or otherwise gives caustic-handling advice that would cause burns.
reference_facts: Raw limestone is calcium carbonate (CaCO3) and is an inert filler, not a cementing binder.
Calcining limestone to quicklime (CaO) requires sustained heating to roughly 850-950C (CaCO3 decomposes at ~825C at 1 atm; traditional limekilns ran near ~900C), driving off CO2.
Quicklime (CaO) + water -> calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)2); this slaking reaction is strongly exothermic and caustic.
Lime mortar gains strength slowly by carbonation: Ca(OH)2 reabsorbs CO2 to re-form CaCO3 over weeks; it must stay damp and is not a 'quick set'.
Typical lime mortar mix is about 1:2.5-3 lime:sand by volume.

title: Design a fair test to rank three homemade glues by strength and wet resistance
scenario: Situation: The survivor has three unlabelled homemade glues (e.g. hide glue, casein glue, starch paste) and must rank them for building work.
Resources: Scrap wood, cordage/rope, known weights (e.g. filled water containers), and water for soaking.
Constraints: No instruments beyond a way to weigh; needs a repeatable comparison, not a guess.
prompt: I made three different batches of glue and I have no idea which is strongest or which falls apart in the rain. I have scrap wood, some rope, and containers I can fill with water to use as known weights, plus water for soaking. How do I set up a fair side-by-side test to rank the three glues for both dry strength and wet resistance — what variables do I have to keep the same, how many samples per glue, and how do I record the results so the answer means something?
rubric: Specifies making identical test joints for every glue: same wood, same grain direction, same glued overlap area, same clamping pressure and same cure time.
Calls for replicates — at least 3 joints per glue per condition — so a single freak failure does not decide the ranking.
Tests a relevant loading mode named explicitly: lap-shear (pull/hang the joint apart along the glue line) rather than a vague 'see if it holds'.
Tests wet resistance by soaking a duplicate set of cured joints in water for a fixed time (e.g. 1-24 h) then loading them, comparing wet vs dry for the same glue.
Loads joints to failure by hanging known weights and increasing in equal, recorded steps (e.g. add a fixed mass each step) until the joint breaks.
Records the failure load for each joint and averages the replicates per glue and condition.
Controls cure: all joints fully cured the same number of days at similar temperature/humidity before any are tested.
Identifies and notes the failure mode (glue line let go vs wood fibre tore out) — wood failure means the glue is stronger than the wood there.
Names a single variable rule: change only the glue between groups, hold everything else constant, or the comparison is meaningless.
Produces a written results table (glue x dry/wet x average failure load) and picks the winner per use-case from it.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Designs a test that changes joint area, wood, or cure time between glues, or uses one sample each, so the ranking cannot be trusted.
Omits any wet/soak condition despite the explicit 'falls apart in the rain' requirement.
reference_facts: A fair comparison holds all variables constant except the one under test (here, the glue): same substrate, bonded area, surface prep, clamping, cure time and temperature.
Adhesive joints must be tested in a relevant mode (shear or tension) and in the relevant environment (dry and wet); a glue strong dry can be near-zero wet.
Replicates (3+ per condition) are needed because individual joints scatter; the average and the failure mode both carry information.
Wood failure (fibres tear out) rather than glue-line failure indicates the bond is stronger than the wood at that point.

title: Dye cloth with plant dye and an alum/iron mordant
scenario: Situation: A small group wants brightly coloured cloth for ground-to-air signalling and to mark roles.
Resources: Local plants (onion skins, walnut hulls, madder/bedstraw roots, berries), wood ash, alum if found, a rusty iron pot or scrap iron, salt, cotton/wool cloth, a fire, and pots.
Constraints: No commercial dyes or fixatives. Colour must survive rain and washing.
prompt: I want to dye cloth bright, lasting colours so we can use it for signalling and to mark who does what. I have local plants (onion skins, walnut hulls, madder roots, berries), wood ash, salt, an old rusty iron pot, and maybe some alum. How do I get the colour out of the plants and make it actually bind to the cloth so it doesn't wash out in the first rain? How much mordant do I use, what does an iron pot do to the colour versus alum, and how do I test that it's colourfast before I trust it?
rubric: States that wool and other protein/animal fibres take natural dyes far more readily than cotton/linen (cellulose), which need stronger or repeated mordanting.
Specifies pre-mordanting cloth with alum at roughly 10-20% of the dry fibre weight (e.g. ~15 g alum per 100 g fibre) dissolved in warm water, simmered ~1 hour, before dyeing.
Explains that an iron mordant ('saddening') darkens and dulls colours (greys/browns/blacks) while alum keeps colours bright/clear, so an iron pot will mute the result.
Warns to use only a small amount of iron (iron over-mordanting rots/embrittles fibres, especially wool, making cloth tender and prone to tearing).
Gives an extraction method: chop/crush plant material, cover with water, simmer ~1 hour, strain out solids to make a dyebath.
Instructs entering wetted (not dry) cloth into the dyebath and simmering ~140-180F / 60-80C (below a hard boil for wool to avoid felting) for ~30-60 min, then cooling slowly.
Notes salt and vinegar/ash are NOT true mordants for most plant dyes and give poor wash-fastness on their own; a metal mordant (alum/iron) is what bonds the dye.
Identifies that walnut hulls (and other tannin/bark dyes) are substantive and bind reasonably without a separate mordant, unlike berry 'stains' which fade fast.
Gives a colourfastness test: rub a dried, rinsed swatch with a damp white cloth (crock test) and/or wash a swatch, checking for bleeding before trusting the batch.
Names a concrete safety control: simmer dye/mordant pots outdoors or well-ventilated, avoid breathing alum/dye dust, and do not reuse a dedicated dye pot for cooking food.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Claims salt or vinegar alone will make plant dye permanently colourfast on cotton (it will wash out).
Recommends a large excess of iron as a mordant without warning that it rots/weakens the fibre.
reference_facts: Alum (potassium aluminium sulphate) is the standard low-tech mordant; typical dose ~10-20% of dry fibre weight (commonly cited ~15%).
Iron (ferrous) mordant saddens/darkens colours and, in excess, degrades cellulose and especially protein fibres, making cloth brittle.
Protein fibres (wool, silk) bond natural dyes far better than cellulose fibres (cotton, linen).
Tannin-rich materials (walnut hulls, oak galls, bark) are substantive dyes that bind without a separate metal mordant; berry pigments are fugitive and fade.
Wool should be dyed below a rolling boil (~60-80C) to avoid felting; sudden temperature change also felts wool.
A crock (rub) test with a damp white cloth and a wash test reveal poor colourfastness before committing a whole batch.

title: Forge nails in quantity with a nail header
scenario: Situation: A survivor needs hundreds of nails for a building project and has only scrap mild-steel rod and a basic forge.
Resources: Charcoal/coal forge, hammer, anvil with a hardy hole and edge, scrap mild-steel rod of suitable diameter.
Constraint: Needs reasonable throughput and consistent sizing, not perfection.
prompt: I need to make hundreds of nails for a building project and all I have is scrap mild-steel rod and my forge with a hammer and anvil. What's the fast, repeatable way to forge nails so I'm not at it for months? How do I make and use a nail header so all the nails come out roughly the same length and head shape?
rubric: Says to use mild steel (not high-carbon) and that nails are NOT heat-treated/hardened -- they just need to be forged to shape and left as-forged.

Describes making a nail header: a hardened steel plate/block with a hole slightly smaller than the nail shank, often set over the hardy hole or held in hand, so the shank drops through and the head is upset on top.
Specifies forging at a bright orange/yellow heat and drawing the rod end down to a tapered point (4-sided/square taper) by rotating and hammering on the anvil.
Says to nick/notch the rod just above the taper (over the anvil edge or hardy/hot-cut) to length, so the part-formed nail can be snapped off into the header rather than cut each one fully free.

Describes setting the hot shank down through the header hole until the shoulder/taper seats, then upsetting/spreading the protruding stub into a head with a few angled hammer blows (e.g. 3-4 blows around the rim for a rose head).
Tells the user to keep the header hole sized to the shank so the head seats consistently, and to work several nails per heat to use each heat efficiently.
Gives a way to standardise length: forge against a marked/scribed length on the anvil or a stop, or use the header depth as a gauge, so heads and lengths repeat.

Gives a realistic throughput figure: an unskilled smith might manage on the order of tens of nails per hour (a skilled nailer historically ~hundreds/hour); output is limited by heating cycles, so it warns hundreds of nails is hours-to-days of work, not minutes.
Includes a quality check: rejects nails that are bent, cold-shut/cracked, or have off-centre heads, and tests a sample by driving it into scrap to confirm it doesn't bend over or crack.

Identifies the main hazards (hot metal, scale/sparks, fire, eye injury, header/anvil fly-offs) and the carbon-monoxide hazard of an enclosed forge, with eye protection and ventilation, plus a stop trigger: stop and reheat once the steel drops to dull red rather than hammering cold steel.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Tells the user to quench/harden the nails like a blade (mild-steel nails should be left as-forged), or claims throughput far beyond reality (e.g. 'thousands per hour by hand').
Advises running the forge in an enclosed/unventilated space (carbon monoxide hazard).
reference_facts: Hand-forged nails are made from mild steel and are left as-forged (not hardened/tempered).
Process: draw a square taper to a point, notch to length over the anvil edge, snap the shank into a nail header, then upset the protruding end into a head with a few blows.
A nail header is a plate/block with a hole slightly smaller than the shank so the head can be formed on top while the shank hangs through.
Forging heat for steel is roughly bright orange to yellow (~900-1200C); stop working below dull red to avoid cold-working cracks.
Throughput is heat-cycle limited: a beginner manages roughly tens of nails per hour; historically skilled nailers produced on the order of hundreds per hour.

title: From raw fleece to woven wool blanket
scenario: Situation: A survivor with no textile experience needs a warm wool blanket before winter and has a raw greasy sheep fleece plus wood to make tools.
Resources: Raw fleece, water, mild soap, wood for tools, time.
Constraint: Must make their own spinning and weaving tools; no commercial yarn or loom.
prompt: Winter is coming and I need a warm wool blanket, but I've never made textiles. I have a big greasy raw sheep fleece and some wood to make tools. Walk me from raw fleece to a finished blanket: how do I clean and prepare the wool, what simple tools do I make to spin yarn and weave cloth, and how do I make the yarn and weave strong enough that it doesn't fall apart?
rubric: Skirting/sorting: pick out and discard the heavily soiled, matted, dung-tagged edges of the fleece first and sort for the cleaner usable wool.

Scouring/washing: wash the grease (lanolin) out in HOT-but-not-boiling water with a little soap, soaking rather than agitating, and warns that agitation + heat + sudden temperature changes will FELT/mat the wool and ruin it.
Drying and teasing: dry the washed wool fully, then tease/pick it open by hand to loosen the locks before carding.
Carding: make carders (boards set with bent nails/teeth or use teasel/comb) and card the wool to align fibres into a fluffy rolag/batt ready to spin.
Spinning tool: describes making a drop spindle (a weighted whorl on a shaft) -- the simplest workable tool -- and notes a spinning wheel is faster if buildable.
Spinning technique: draft out a thin even amount of fibre and add twist with the spindle so the fibres lock together into yarn; consistent thickness and enough twist give strength.

Warp vs weft: spin the WARP yarn (lengthwise, under tension on the loom) tighter/stronger and more even, and the weft (crosswise) can be softer/loftier for warmth; optionally ply two singles together for a stronger, more balanced yarn.

Loom and weaving: build a simple frame loom or warp-weighted loom, set up the warp under even tension, and weave plain weave (over-one/under-one) with consistent beating so the cloth is even, not loose or buckled.
Finishing/fulling: after weaving, wash and gently full (agitate in warm soapy water) the cloth so the fibres mesh slightly, which closes the weave and makes it warmer and more durable; then dry flat.

Verification/quality: check that the warp tension is even and the yarn doesn't pull apart under hand tension before weaving; reject/re-spin yarn that is so loosely twisted it drafts apart, since weak warp will break repeatedly on the loom.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Advises washing/scouring raw wool by vigorous agitation in hot water (or boiling and stirring), which felts the fleece and destroys it.
Omits adding twist when spinning (e.g. says to just lay or glue fibres together), so the result is not real yarn and has no strength.
reference_facts: Raw fleece is coated in lanolin (grease) and dirt; it is scoured in hot (not boiling) water with soap by soaking, avoiding agitation.
Wool felts/mats irreversibly when subjected to heat + agitation + temperature shock; this must be avoided during scouring but is used deliberately when fulling finished cloth.
Carding aligns fibres for spinning; a drop spindle (whorl + shaft) is the simplest spinning tool.
Yarn strength comes from drafting an even amount of fibre and adding sufficient twist; plying two singles balances and strengthens yarn.
Warp yarn must be stronger and more tightly spun than weft because it is held under tension on the loom; plain weave is over-one/under-one.
Fulling (controlled agitation in warm soapy water) after weaving densifies wool cloth for warmth and durability.

title: Identify scrap steel by spark testing on a grinder
scenario: Situation: The user is sorting mystery scrap to pick stock: high-carbon steel for a knife, tougher mild steel for a prybar.

Resources: An angle grinder or bench grinding wheel, a known steel file and a known mild-steel nail as references, eye protection, and a heap of mixed scrap (mild steel, high-carbon steel, cast iron, possibly stainless).
Constraints: No lab; identification is by spark stream alone.
prompt: I have a pile of mystery scrap metal and an angle grinder, and I need to pick the right pieces: high-carbon steel for a knife blade, and something tougher and less brittle like mild steel for a heavy prybar. How can I tell high-carbon steel from mild steel, cast iron, and stainless just by the sparks when I grind them? What exactly am I looking for in the spark stream, how do I use a known file and a known nail to calibrate my eye, and what safety precautions matter while grinding?
rubric: Explains the governing principle: carbon content drives spark 'bursting' - more carbon produces more numerous, brighter, forking starburst explosions in the spark stream.
Mild/low-carbon steel: long, straight, yellowish-white spark streaks with few sparse forks/bursts (mostly straight lines).
High-carbon steel (knife stock): a shorter, bushier, bright white spark stream with many repeated branching starbursts/explosions starting near the wheel.
Cast iron: very short, low-volume, dull red sparks near the wheel that turn straw/yellow with small repeating sparklers, and an overall short faint stream distinct from steel.

Stainless steel: a moderate-length stream with few sparklers and little bursting compared with plain high-carbon (also resists rust and is non-magnetic if austenitic) - distinct from the heavy starbursts of high-carbon.
Calibration: grind the KNOWN file (high-carbon, many bursts) and the KNOWN nail (mild, few bursts) first to anchor what each spark pattern looks like, then compare the unknowns.
Maps the recommendation correctly: pick the high-carbon (file-like, bursty) piece for the knife blade, and the mild-steel (long, few-burst) piece for the tougher, less-brittle prybar.
Notes cast iron is brittle and unsuitable for either a knife edge or a prybar, and should be set aside for both jobs.
Advises grinding with a light, consistent pressure in a shaded spot/against a dark background so the spark colour and bursts are visible and comparable.

Names concrete safety controls: wear eye protection/face shield (and ideally a mask for dust), clear flammables/fuel away (sparks ignite fires), check the wheel is sound, and beware hot metal and grinding sparks to skin/clothing.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
States that high-carbon steel makes long straight sparks with few bursts while mild steel bursts a lot (the relationship inverted).
Omits eye protection / fire-hazard control while grinding.
reference_facts: Spark bursting increases with carbon content: high-carbon steel = many bright forking starbursts; mild steel = long straight streaks with few bursts.
Cast iron = very short, dull red, low-volume non-forking sparks beginning at the wheel.
Stainless steel = shorter orange sparks with little forking; austenitic stainless is non-magnetic and rust-resistant.
High-carbon steel (e.g. an old file, springs) suits a hardenable knife edge; mild steel is tougher and less brittle for a prybar.
Cast iron is brittle and unsuitable for a knife edge or a prybar.
A known file (high-carbon) and known nail (mild) serve as calibration references for the spark patterns.

title: Make a hydraulic (pozzolanic) lime mortar that sets underwater
scenario: **Situation:** A person needs mortar for a cistern/pier that stays wet and must set hard underwater.
**Resources:** Quicklime, sand, and either volcanic ash OR crushed/ground fired clay brick or pottery. No Portland cement.
**Goal:** A waterproof, hydraulic-setting mortar.
prompt: I'm building a cistern that will hold water, and I need mortar that sets hard and stays hard underwater. I have quicklime, sand, and either some volcanic ash or old fired clay bricks I can crush to powder. I don't have any modern cement. Why won't plain lime-and-sand mortar work when it's permanently wet? How do I use the ash or crushed brick to make a mortar that actually sets underwater, what mix ratios should I use, and how do I cure it?
rubric: Explains that ordinary lime+sand mortar is NON-hydraulic (air lime): it sets by reacting with CO2 from the air (carbonation), so it cannot cure when permanently submerged and stays soft/dissolves.
Identifies the fix as adding a pozzolan — finely ground volcanic ash OR crushed fired clay brick/pottery dust (cocciopesto) — to make the mortar hydraulic.

Explains the chemistry: pozzolans contain reactive (amorphous) silica/alumina that reacts with the lime and water to form insoluble calcium-silicate/aluminate hydrates that set chemically, even underwater.
States the pozzolan must be FIRED/reactive and ground FINE; ordinary unfired clay or coarse aggregate is not pozzolanic — only burnt clay (brick/tile/pottery) or true volcanic ash works.
Gives a mix ratio roughly 1 part lime : 2–3 parts pozzolan/ash by volume (leaner ~1:2 for the most demanding underwater work), plus sand as needed.
Covers preparing the lime safely: slaking quicklime in water (adding lime to water) is strongly exothermic and spatters caustic slurry — describes care, or using already-slaked lime putty.
Identifies the caustic burn/eye hazard of quicklime and slaked lime and the need to protect skin and eyes and avoid breathing the dust.
Describes mixing to a workable paste with minimum water, working it well, and using promptly before it stiffens.
Describes the curing requirement: keep it damp/protected (or submerged) while it cures — do NOT let a hydraulic lime dry out fast; it gains strength slowly over weeks.
Gives a concrete test: make a small sample, let it cure, then leave it submerged in water for some days and confirm it stays hard and doesn't slump or wash away before relying on it.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends plain lime+sand (air-lime) mortar for the permanently-wet/underwater application.
Adds the wrong additive (e.g. raw unfired clay, or plain sand only) as the 'pozzolan', or claims plain lime mortar sets by drying.
reference_facts: Air (non-hydraulic) lime sets by carbonation with atmospheric CO2 and cannot cure underwater; hydraulic set requires a pozzolanic reaction.

Pozzolans (volcanic ash, or crushed fired clay brick/tile/pottery = cocciopesto) supply reactive amorphous silica/alumina that reacts with lime + water to form insoluble C-S-H / C-A-S-H, giving an underwater-setting (hydraulic) mortar.
Vitruvius/Roman practice used roughly 1 part lime to 2–3 parts pozzolana (about 1:2 for marine/underwater work).
Only fired/calcined clay is pozzolanic; raw unfired clay is not.
Slaking quicklime is strongly exothermic and the material is caustic (lime burns); eye and skin protection are required.
Hydraulic lime mortar must be kept damp and cures/gains strength slowly over weeks.

title: Make and apply pine-pitch sealant for roof seams and buckets
scenario: Situation: Survivor needs to seal roof seams and waterproof wooden buckets.
Resources: Pine resin (sap), charcoal/ash powder, plant fibres, a heat source, basic tools.
Constraint: Hot molten resin is a flash/burn fire hazard; the cured sealant must not be too brittle and crack off.
prompt: I need to waterproof my roof seams and some wooden buckets. I have plenty of pine resin, some charcoal/ash powder, and plant fibres. How do I turn these into a durable sealant that won't just crack off? What proportions do I use, how do I cook it without setting it on fire or burning myself, and how do I apply it and test that it's actually watertight?
rubric: Specifies melting the pine resin gently over LOW/indirect heat (e.g. a water bath or embers, double-boiler style), never boiling it hard over a roaring flame.
States the key fix for cracking: pure hardened resin is brittle, so add a softener/plasticiser — fat, tallow, beeswax or oil — typically a small fraction (roughly 10-25%) to keep the pitch flexible.
Specifies adding powdered charcoal/ash (and/or fine fibre) as a filler/temper, roughly up to ~1 part filler to a few parts resin, to add body and reduce brittleness and runniness.
Specifies adding chopped plant fibre for seam/crack filling to bridge gaps and resist cracking (a fibre-reinforced 'pitch glue').
Warns that pine pitch is thermoplastic: it softens and can run in hot sun / near heat, so it suits seams and buckets but not surfaces that get very hot.
Gives an ordered sequence: clean and DRY the surface, warm the surface/joint, apply the molten pitch into the seam, press fibre in if needed, smooth and let cool/harden.
Names the fire hazards explicitly: resin vapour is flammable and can flash; keep a lid and water/sand nearby, melt away from open flame, and never add water to hot pitch (it spatters).
Names the burn hazard: molten pitch sticks to skin and keeps burning; wear cover, do not overheat, and for a skin splash cool with water and do NOT peel it off.
Specifies a stop condition: if the resin smokes heavily, darkens/chars, or catches, remove from heat immediately (smother, do not throw water).
Gives a watertightness test: fill the sealed bucket / wet the roof seam and look for drips or damp on the far side after standing, and re-apply pitch where it weeps.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Tells the user to heat the resin directly over an open high flame / boil it hard (real flash-fire and burn hazard).
Tells the user to throw water onto hot/burning pitch or to peel molten pitch off burned skin.
Recommends pure unmodified resin with no plasticiser/filler and claims it will not crack.
reference_facts: Pine pitch sealant = pine resin melted and blended with a plasticiser (fat/tallow/beeswax/oil, ~10-25%) and a powdered filler (charcoal/ash) and/or fibre to reduce brittleness; pure resin is brittle and cracks.
Pitch is thermoplastic: it must be heated to apply and softens/runs in heat, so it is unsuitable for very hot surfaces.
Resin must be melted over low/indirect heat (water bath/embers); its vapour is flammable and can flash; never add water to hot pitch.
Molten pitch adheres to skin and continues to burn; cool a splash with water and do not peel it off.
Surfaces must be clean and dry before applying; watertightness is verified by a fill/wet test and re-sealing leaks.

title: Make casein (milk) glue for woodworking and know its limits
scenario: Situation: Survivor has surplus milk and needs a strong adhesive for furniture/woodworking joints.
Resources: Milk, vinegar or lemon juice (acid), wood ash or lime (alkali), a cloth filter, a heat source. No modern glues.
Constraint: Needs to know the recipe, working/clamp time, and that it is not waterproof.
prompt: I have surplus milk and heard it makes a strong woodworking glue. I have vinegar and some wood ash (and could make lime). How do I actually make the glue — what do I add and in what order — and how do I use it on wood joints? What's the clamp/working time, and what is it no good for, like anything that gets wet?
rubric: Specifies using skim/low-fat milk (or skimming the fat), because fat weakens the bond.
Step 1 — curdle the milk with an acid (vinegar or lemon juice), gently warmed (~50-60 C), to separate curds (casein) from the watery whey.
Step 2 — strain off and rinse the curds through the cloth, discarding the whey, to get the casein protein.
Step 3 — add a small amount of alkali (slaked lime / calcium hydroxide, or wood-ash lye) to the moist curds; it dissolves/peptises the casein into a smooth glue.
Explains the chemistry: the alkali converts casein into caseinate (calcium caseinate with lime), which is the actual adhesive binder.
States that lime (calcium caseinate) gives the more water-resistant, durable bond, whereas a milder alkali like wood-ash/soda gives a strong but more water-sensitive glue.
States the application: brush the glue thinly onto clean, well-fitted wood surfaces and CLAMP, because casein glue needs clamping pressure and bonds best on close-fitting joints.
States the timing: it has a limited pot/working life (use within hours — the limed mix gels fast) and needs many hours/overnight clamped to set, with full strength developing over a day or more.
States the limitation clearly: casein glue is NOT waterproof — it softens/fails when soaked and can grow mould if kept damp; keep glued items dry, not for submerged/outdoor-wet use.
Names a hazard/QC control: lime and ash lye are caustic (protect skin/eyes); and verify the bond by testing a scrap joint to failure (wood should tear before the glue line) before relying on it.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Omits the alkali (lime/ash) step, leaving plain curds that do not form a proper caseinate glue.
Claims casein/milk glue is waterproof or fine for items that get wet or are stored damp.
Tells the user the glue needs no clamping / will bond strongly on loose or gap-filled joints.
reference_facts: Casein glue: curdle skim milk with acid (~50-60 C), strain/rinse curds, then add an alkali (slaked lime / calcium hydroxide, or wood-ash lye) which converts casein to caseinate glue.
Lime (calcium hydroxide) gives calcium caseinate, which becomes largely water-insoluble on drying (more durable); milder alkalis (baking soda/ash) give a strong but more water-sensitive bond.
Casein glue requires close-fitting joints and clamping pressure; it has a short pot life (the limed mix gels within hours) and sets over hours, gaining full strength over a day or more.
Casein glue is not waterproof: it softens when soaked and can mould if kept damp; glued items must be kept dry.
Lime and wood-ash lye are caustic alkalis; protect skin and eyes.
Fat impairs casein bonding, so skim/low-fat milk is used.

title: Make durable lime-casein paint for a clinic wall
scenario: Situation: A clinic/dairy interior needs a sanitary, washable wall coating; plain limewash chalks off and rubs onto clothing.
Resources: Slaked lime (lime putty or hydrated lime), milk/sour milk/curds (quark), water, earth pigments, brushes, and a bucket.
Constraints: No commercial paint or binders.
prompt: I need to paint the inside of my clinic to keep it clean and washable, but the plain limewash I used rubs off on everyone's clothes and chalks away. I have lime and some sour milk or curds, plus earth pigments. How do I combine the lime and milk into a paint that's far more durable and doesn't flake - what's actually happening chemically that makes it stick? Give me proportions, how to prep the wall and apply it, how long the mixed paint stays usable, and why this is better than plain whitewash.
rubric: Explains the chemistry: the alkaline lime reacts with casein (milk protein) to form calcium caseinate, an insoluble binder/glue that locks the pigment and lime to the wall.
States that once dry this calcium-caseinate film is largely water-insoluble and washable, so it does not chalk/rub off like plain limewash (which is only loosely bound calcium carbonate).
Gives a workable recipe: combine curds/quark or skim/sour milk with slaked lime putty (roughly a few parts curd to one part lime by volume), thin with water, and stir in earth pigment to colour.
Notes the lime must be slaked/hydrated (lime putty or hydrated lime), not raw quicklime, and that the mix turns from curdled to a smooth, slightly translucent paint as the caseinate forms.
States the mixed paint is perishable: it must be used the same day (the wet milk-lime mix rots/sours and smells), even though the dried film is durable.

Gives wall prep: surface must be clean, sound, free of grease/loose old paint and dust, and lime paints bond best to mineral/porous substrates (plaster, masonry); dampening a very dry/absorbent wall first improves adhesion.
Specifies applying in thin, even coats (two or more thin coats rather than one thick), letting each dry, because thick lime coats crack and chalk.
Notes lime's high pH gives the coating antimicrobial/disinfectant properties, which is why it suits a clinic/dairy.
Names earth/mineral pigments (ochres, iron oxides) as lime-stable colourants and warns that many organic dyes are bleached/destroyed by lime's high pH.
Names a concrete safety control: lime is caustic/alkaline - wear eye protection and gloves, avoid skin/eye contact and breathing lime dust, and ventilate the space.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Claims the wet mixed lime-casein paint keeps for a long time (it spoils within a day).
Tells the user to use raw quicklime directly in the paint without slaking it first.
reference_facts: Lime (alkaline) reacts with casein (milk protein) to form calcium caseinate, an insoluble binder.
The dried calcium-caseinate film is water-resistant and washable, unlike plain limewash which chalks off.
Recipe uses slaked lime putty/hydrated lime + curds/quark/sour milk + water + earth pigment; not raw quicklime.
Mixed lime-casein paint must be used the same day; it spoils/rots while wet but is stable once dry.
Lime's high pH is antimicrobial, suiting clinic/dairy use; earth/iron-oxide pigments are lime-fast while many organic dyes are destroyed by the alkali.
Lime is caustic; eye/skin protection and dust avoidance are required.

title: Make fired-clay water jars that don't seep through the walls
scenario: Situation: The survivor needs 10-20 L jars for water storage; current jars 'sweat' and seep water through the walls.
Resources: Local clay and a simple pit/bonfire kiln; no commercial glaze.
Constraints: Earthenware temperatures only (no high-fire vitrification), so leakage must be controlled by clay prep, firing, and post-sealing.
prompt: I'm making big clay jars (about 10-20 litres) to store water, but the ones I've fired so far just seep water through the walls and sweat on the outside. I only have local clay and a simple pit fire — no real glaze. How should I clean and prepare the clay, form and dry the jars, and fire them so they hold water better? And since I can't reach glaze temperatures, what can I do to the fired jars to seal the pores?
rubric: Explains earthenware fired in a pit/bonfire (roughly 600-900C) stays porous because it never vitrifies, so the seeping is inherent and must be reduced by clay prep, firing and sealing — not by thicker walls.
Prepares clay by removing coarse grit/organics: slake in water, sieve/levigate to keep the fine fraction, and wedge/knead to remove air and homogenise.
Adds a modest temper (fine sand or grog ~10-20%) for thermal/drying-crack resistance but warns too much temper increases porosity/leakage.
Builds walls of even thickness and compresses/burnishes the surface (rub leather-hard clay with a smooth stone) to close surface pores before firing.
Dries the jars slowly and evenly (days, out of direct sun/wind, covered loosely) to bone-dry before firing.
Pre-heats/warms the jars gently at the fire edge to drive off remaining moisture before full firing, to avoid steam spalling.
Fires with a slow temperature ramp and the longest, hottest soak the pit allows, then cools slowly to reduce cracking.
Post-seals the fired jar by impregnating the pores — e.g. boiling milk, fat/tallow, beeswax, plant oil, or starch/flour gruel soaked in and heated so it penetrates and clogs the pores.
Offers an alternative seal of pine-pitch/resin lining (melted and swirled inside) for storage of non-potable or cool water, noting flavour/heat limits.
Tests the finished jar by filling with water and standing it on dry ground/cloth for several hours, checking for weeping or a wet ring, before relying on it.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends a lead-based or other toxic glaze/sealant for a water vessel, or lining a drinking-water jar with a toxic substance.
Claims simply firing hotter or making walls thicker will make porous earthenware watertight without any sealing or vitrification.
reference_facts: Earthenware fired below ~1000C does not vitrify and remains porous; pit/bonfire firing typically reaches only ~600-900C.
Levigation (slaking, settling, decanting the fine fraction) yields a smoother, less leaky body than raw dug clay.
Burnishing leather-hard clay with a smooth stone mechanically closes surface pores and reduces seepage.
Porous fired pots are traditionally sealed by soaking/boiling in milk, fat, oil, wax, or starch which penetrate and clog the pores; pitch/resin can line non-cooking vessels.
Lead glazes leach into water/acidic food and are toxic — never use for drinking-water or food vessels.

title: Make food-safe cookpot surfaces without modern glaze
scenario: Situation: The survivor needs clay cooking and storage vessels that are non-toxic, don't leak grease, and don't soak up old food.
Resources: Wood ash, clay slip, burnishing stones, plant oils; no lead or modern glaze chemicals.
Constraints: Must avoid toxic treatments and recognise the limits of porous earthenware for wet food storage.
prompt: I want my clay cooking and storage pots to be safe to eat from, not soak up old food, and not leak grease. I have wood ash, clay slip, smooth burnishing stones, and plant oils — no lead or modern glaze chemicals. What's the best way to treat the surfaces so they're sealed and food-safe? What should I definitely NOT use? And how do I test whether a pot is actually sealed before I cook in it?
rubric: Names lead glazes/lead-bearing materials and any unknown scrap/automotive/painted material as things NOT to use because lead and similar metals leach into food and are toxic.
Recommends burnishing the leather-hard pot with a smooth stone to compress and close surface pores as the primary, fully food-safe surface treatment.
Seasons/seals the fired pot with food-grade organics: rub in plant oil (or fat/milk) and heat the pot so the oil penetrates and polymerises into the pores, or boil milk/starch in it to clog pores.
Warns that an ash glaze needs true high-fire kiln temperatures (well above simple pit firing) to melt and fuse, so it is usually not achievable here — and unfused ash is not a reliable seal.
States porous low-fired earthenware is poor for long-term wet/perishable food storage: it absorbs liquids, harbours bacteria, and is hard to fully sanitise.
Advises thoroughly drying pots between uses, not leaving wet food standing in them, and dedicating porous pots to dry goods or short-term use.
Specifies first heating a new pot gradually (temper it to fire/thermal shock) before cooking, to avoid cracking.
Tests for porosity by weighing/observing a pot dry, then filling with water for a set time and checking for a damp exterior, weight gain, or a wet ring underneath.
Tests grease/seal by wiping cooked grease and checking it wipes clean rather than soaking in and staining.
Gives a stop/reject rule: if a pot still weeps, stains with grease, or retains food odour after sealing, do not use it for wet/perishable food.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends a lead glaze, galvanised/zinc, painted, or unknown metal/scrap coating for a food-contact surface.
Claims a simple pit-fired ash 'glaze' or a single oil wipe makes a porous pot fully sealed and safe for long-term wet food storage.
reference_facts: Lead glazes leach into food, especially acidic food, and are toxic; never use lead, galvanised/zinc, or unknown painted/plated coatings on food surfaces.
Burnishing leather-hard clay compresses and closes surface pores and is fully food-safe.
Seasoning with food-grade oil/fat/milk that is heated into the pores, or boiling milk/starch in the pot, clogs pores and reduces grease soak — a food-safe seal.
True ash glazes require high-fire temperatures (well above ordinary pit/bonfire firing) to melt and fuse to the body.
Low-fired porous earthenware absorbs liquids and harbours bacteria; it is unsuitable for long-term wet/perishable food storage and should be dried fully between uses.

title: Make insulating (burn-out) firebrick for a forge
scenario: Situation: Survivor's clay forge will not reach melting heat because the dense clay walls conduct heat away too fast.
Resources: Plenty of clay, a large pile of sawdust (or rice husks), and water. Can mould and fire bricks.
Constraint: Needs lighter, insulating bricks to hold heat in.
prompt: My clay forge won't get hot enough to melt metal — the heat just leaks out through the dense clay walls. I have plenty of clay, a huge pile of fine sawdust, and water. How do I make bricks that trap heat inside instead of conducting it away? Tell me the mix proportions, how to mould and dry them, how to fire them, where to use them in the forge, and the downsides I should plan for.
rubric: States the goal is insulating firebrick: a porous, lightweight brick rather than dense brick.
Specifies blending a high proportion of sawdust into the clay — on the order of 30-50% by volume of combustible filler.
Explains that during firing the sawdust burns out, leaving a network of small voids/pores throughout the brick.
States the physics: trapped still air in the pores is a poor conductor, so the porous brick insulates far better than dense clay (which conducts heat away).
Gives the sequence: mix clay+sawdust+water to a stiff plastic mass, mould to uniform bricks, then dry SLOWLY because high filler content means high shrinkage and crack risk.
Specifies firing the dried bricks (bisque) so the sawdust fully combusts and the clay sinters into a coherent porous body.
Specifies using the porous insulating bricks as the inner hot-face lining / forge body, optionally with a denser, more durable layer where mechanical wear or direct flame impingement occurs.
States the key trade-off: insulating bricks are mechanically weaker, softer and more friable than dense brick, and less resistant to abrasion/slag.
Notes a uniform mix and uniform brick thickness, plus slow drying out of wind/sun, to avoid warping and cracking.
Mentions alternative pore-forming burn-out fillers (e.g. rice husks, chopped straw, charcoal/coke dust) if sawdust runs short.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends making the walls denser/thicker solid clay to insulate (the opposite of what reduces conduction).
Adds a non-combustible or hazardous filler that does not burn out (e.g. plastic foam left in, expecting it to vanish cleanly) as the primary method, leaving toxic residue or solid inclusions.
reference_facts: Insulating firebrick is made porous so trapped still air (a poor thermal conductor) reduces heat loss; dense refractory conducts heat faster.
A common low-tech method adds 30-50% by volume of a burn-out filler (sawdust, rice husk, ground charcoal) to clay; the filler combusts during firing leaving voids.
High filler/porosity bricks are lighter and more insulating but mechanically weaker and softer than dense firebrick.
High-shrinkage, high-filler bricks must be dried slowly and uniformly to avoid cracking and warping before firing.
Rice husks, chopped straw and charcoal/coke dust are alternative burn-out pore-formers.

title: Make low-smoke oil lamps and tallow candles for indoor use
scenario: Situation: A clinic and a workshop need safe indoor lighting after dark.
Resources: Tallow, beeswax, or plant oil; small jars/tins; cotton/linen rag, plant fibre, or twisted cordage for wicks; a fire to melt fat/wax.
Constraints: Must minimise soot/smoke (clinic air quality) and fire risk in occupied rooms.
prompt: We need light in a clinic and a workshop after dark. I can get tallow, beeswax, or plant oil, plus small jars and tins, and cotton rag or plant fibre for wicks. Should I make candles or oil lamps, and how do I actually build each? How do I size and prepare a wick so it burns bright without belching black smoke, and what do I do to stop these from setting the building on fire while people sleep nearby? Which fuel burns cleanest indoors?
rubric: Explains the oil lamp works by capillary action: a wick draws liquid fuel (plant oil) up to the flame, which burns the fuel vapour, not the wick itself; the wick should char but not be consumed quickly.
States that a wick too large/too high produces an oversized smoky flame and soot, while a wick trimmed short and snug gives a clean flame; trim the charred tip regularly.
Recommends a natural-fibre wick (cotton/linen, twisted/braided) and warns against synthetic or treated materials (toxic fumes) and against very loose fibre that wicks too fast and smokes.

For candles, explains tallow needs a wick sized for its lower melt point and that beeswax burns cleaner/harder than tallow (tallow can smell and smoke more); plant oil is best in a lamp, not a moulded candle.
Gives a candle method: repeated dipping of the wick in melted fat/wax, or pouring into a mould/tin, building up layers and letting each set.
Notes that an enclosed lamp/lantern (glass chimney or a jar) steadies airflow, gives cleaner combustion and protects the flame from drafts.

Gives a concrete fire-safety control: place lamps/candles on a stable, non-tipping, non-combustible base, away from bedding/curtains/loose clothing, and never leave a flame unattended or burning while everyone sleeps.
Warns about carbon monoxide / oxygen depletion: any open flame consumes oxygen and emits CO, so keep the clinic/workshop ventilated, especially with multiple lamps.
States that the cleanest indoor fuels are beeswax and refined plant oil; raw tallow and unrefined fats smoke and smell more, so favour them where air quality matters (clinic).
Gives a verification/adjust step: if the flame smokes or soots the jar, lower/trim the wick until the flame is steady and bright with little visible smoke.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends a volatile/explosive fuel (petrol/gasoline, alcohol) in an open wick lamp for indoor lighting.
Omits any fire-safety control (stable base, clearance from combustibles, not leaving unattended).
reference_facts: An oil lamp burns by capillary action: the wick lifts liquid fuel to the flame, which burns the vapour; the wick chars slowly.
Over-long/over-thick wicks cause large smoky sooty flames; trimming the wick short gives a clean flame.
Natural fibre (cotton/linen) wicks are correct; synthetic/treated fibres emit toxic fumes.
Beeswax burns cleaner and harder than tallow; refined plant oil suits a lamp; raw tallow smokes/smells more.
Open flames consume oxygen and produce carbon monoxide; ventilation is required indoors.
Petrol/gasoline and alcohol are too volatile for open-wick indoor lamps (flash/explosion risk).

title: Make strong cordage and rope from nettle and bark fibre
scenario: Survivor needs ~30 m of strong rope for hauling and shelter guy lines and has no manufactured cord.
Available fibre: nettle stems, tree bark (e.g. inner bark/bast), and flax-like plants.
Tools: a knife and a simple wooden spindle.
prompt: I need about 30 m of strong rope for hauling supplies and shelter guy lines, but I have no cordage. I have nettles, tree bark, and some flax-like plants, plus a knife. How do I prepare the fibres and twist them into a rope that won't snap under load, and how do I make it that long without leaving weak spots where I join sections? How do I check it's actually strong before I trust it?
rubric: Harvests fibre from the long bast/stem fibres (nettle stalk, inner bark, flax-like stems), not the brittle outer skin or woody core, to get long strong strands.
Retts and/or strips the stems (soaking/drying to rot away the soft tissue, or scraping/crushing) to free clean long fibres, then dries them.
Splits fibres into fine, even bundles and removes short/broken pieces, because uniform long fibres give uniform strength.
Twists each bundle hard in one direction to form a yarn/strand (e.g. clockwise / 'Z' twist), so the twist binds the fibres and friction carries the load.
Plies two or more strands together with the OPPOSITE twist (counter-twist, e.g. 'S' ply over Z strands) so the rope holds itself together and does not unravel - the reverse-wrap principle.
Lengthens strands by splicing in new fibre BEFORE the old runs out: overlap and taper the new bundle into the existing one over a long distance so the join is gradual.
Staggers the joins so no two splices fall at the same point along the rope, avoiding a single weak cross-section.
States that abrupt joins or knots create stress concentrations / weak points and reduce strength, so they are avoided within the rope body.

Includes a proof/quality check: progressively load-test the finished rope (well below where people stand under it) and inspect for thin spots, slipping plies, or pulling-apart at splices before trusting it.
Stores the rope dry and off the ground, because natural fibre rots, weakens, and mildews when kept damp.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Lengthens the rope by tying butt knots between sections (creating weak points) instead of overlapping/tapered splices.
Plies strands in the SAME direction as their twist (no counter-twist), so the rope unravels and will not hold.
reference_facts: Strong plant cordage comes from long bast/stem fibres, freed by retting/stripping and dried.
The reverse-wrap principle: strands are twisted one way, then plied together the opposite way, so the counter-twist locks the rope together.
Strands are lengthened by overlapping/tapering (splicing) new fibre in before the old ends, with joins staggered along the length.
Abrupt joins and knots create stress concentrations and reduce rope strength.
Natural-fibre rope must be stored dry to prevent rot and strength loss.

title: Making isinglass glue from fish swim bladders
scenario: Situation: A bowyer is building a sinew-and-horn composite bow and wants the strongest, most flexible water-based glue available.
Resources: Fish offal including the swim bladders (sounds) of caught fish; clean water and a gentle heat source / double boiler.
Goal: Turn the bladders into isinglass glue and understand how it compares to hide glue.
prompt: I'm building a composite bow and need the strongest, most flexible glue I can make. I have fish guts including the little silvery air bladders. How do I turn those bladders into glue, step by step? Cover cleaning and drying, exactly how hot I can take it without ruining it, how I apply it, whether it can be softened again later, and is it actually better than the glue boiled from animal hides and skins?
rubric: Identifies the raw material as the fish swim bladder (sound), with sturgeon being the prized source but cod/catfish/other fish usable, and notes the product is collagen-rich isinglass.
Directs cleaning the bladders: slit, rinse out blood and the inner membrane/slime, and scrape, because blood and tissue rot and weaken the glue.
Says to dry the cleaned bladders thoroughly (they keep for a long time dried) and to cut/shred or pound them into small pieces before use.
Directs soaking/swelling the dried pieces in cool or warm water first, then dissolving them with gentle heat in a double boiler / water bath rather than direct flame.
Specifies a low temperature (roughly 45-60 C, well below boiling), warning that boiling or overheating breaks down the collagen and destroys the glue's strength.
States that isinglass is a strong, low-viscosity, comparatively flexible glue that works well in thin coats and for composites/laminations (good for the sinew/horn bow).
Explains the glue is water-reversible/thermoplastic: a dried joint can be softened again with warmth and moisture, which aids reglueing and repairs.
Compares to hide/skin glue: isinglass is generally purer, higher-tack and more flexible (less brittle), at the cost of being more laborious to prepare; hide glue is the more common substitute.
Notes application while warm and liquid onto clean, well-fitted surfaces under clamping/binding pressure, working before it gels, since it sets/gels as it cools.
Covers storage/hygiene: keep prepared glue covered and use promptly because the wet glue spoils (rots/moulds) within days; store the surplus as the dried bladders, not as standing liquid.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Directs boiling the bladders hard to dissolve them (destroys collagen and ruins glue strength).
Claims isinglass is permanently waterproof/irreversible (it is water-soluble and reversible) in a way that would mislead the bow build.
reference_facts: Isinglass is collagen from dried fish swim bladders (sounds); sturgeon bladders give the highest-grade glue, but other fish work.
It is dissolved in warm water with gentle heat (double boiler ~45-60 C); boiling/overheating degrades the collagen and weakens the glue.
Isinglass has higher adhesion, lower viscosity and more flexibility than typical hide/rabbit-skin glue, owing to fewer collagen cross-links, making it valued for composites and conservation.
Like other animal/collagen glues it is thermoplastic and water-reversible: dried joints soften with heat/moisture.
Collagen glues set by gelling on cooling and must be applied warm; the prepared wet glue spoils in days and is best stored dried.
Blood and soft tissue must be cleaned off the bladders before drying or the glue rots and weakens.

title: Mix and test cob (clay/sand/straw) for shelter walls
scenario: **Situation:** A survivor is building monolithic earthen (cob) walls for a shelter in a wet climate.
**Resources:** Clay-heavy subsoil, sand, straw, water, feet/tarp for mixing.
**Goal:** Get a mix that doesn't crack badly or crumble, and protect the wall from rain.
prompt: I'm building shelter walls out of cob, mud mixed with sand and straw, but I'm not sure my mix is right. I have clay-heavy subsoil, sand, and straw. What proportions should I aim for, and how do I test a small batch to tell if it'll crack too much or crumble apart when it dries? It rains a lot here, so also tell me how to keep the walls from washing away.
rubric: Identifies the three components' roles: clay = binder/glue, sand (aggregate) = bulk that resists shrinkage cracking, straw = tensile reinforcement that ties the mass together and limits crack propagation.
