Aluminium solidification (volumetric) shrinkage is roughly 6% (about 3.5-8% range cited for cast Al alloys), requiring a riser/feeder and slightly oversize pattern.
Green sand is typically ~85-90% sand, ~5-11% bentonite clay, ~3-5% water; correct temper = a squeezed lump holds shape and snaps cleanly.
Molten aluminium contacting water/moisture causes a steam (and possible hydrogen) explosion; all tooling and moulds must be dry/preheated.
Gating order: sprue -> runner -> gate -> cavity, with a riser at the thickest section; vents let mould gas/steam escape (green sand is permeable).
Dross/oxide must be skimmed and the melt fluxed before pouring to avoid inclusions and cold shuts.

title: Set up a 6-person workflow to make clay roof tiles in two weeks
scenario: Situation: A shed roof must be tiled within two weeks using locally dug clay.
Resources: 6 people, local clay, water, wood/charcoal fuel, and the ability to build a basic updraught kiln.
Constraints: Tiles must be flat, weatherproof, and consistent; the main failure modes are warping and cracking from uneven drying and firing.
prompt: I need to roof a small shed with clay tiles in the next two weeks. I have five other people to help (six of us total), local clay, water, plenty of wood, and we can build a basic kiln. How should we organise the workflow so we actually produce enough usable tiles? What moulds and drying racks do we need, how thick should the tiles be, how do we dry and fire them so they don't all warp or crack, and how do we cull the bad ones?
rubric: Splits the 6 people into parallel stations (clay digging/prep, moulding, drying-rack management, kiln building/firing) and notes drying is the rate-limiting step that must start on day 1.
Specifies preparing clay by removing stones/organics and wedging out air, adding sand/grog temper (~10-20%) to cut drying shrinkage and warping.
Uses a simple wooden mould/frame of fixed size with a strike-off stick so every tile is the same footprint and uniform thickness (~15-20 mm).
Forms a nib/lug or nail hole and, for curved tiles, drapes the slab over a former so the curvature is consistent.
Dries tiles flat on level, ventilated racks, turning/flipping them and shielding from direct sun and wind so both faces dry evenly (warping comes from one face drying faster).
Allows days of slow air-drying to bone-dry, sand-dusting or cloth between tile and mould so they release without distortion.
Pre-heats/water-smokes the kiln slowly to drive off residual moisture before raising heat, to prevent steam explosions.
Fires earthenware up to roughly 900-1000C with a gradual ramp and soak, then cools slowly (do not open the hot kiln) to avoid thermal-shock cracks.
States 'uniform thickness, slow even drying, and a controlled ramp matter more than maximum temperature' and that overfiring/uneven heat warps tiles.

Defines a rejection/QC check: cull tiles that are warped (rock on a flat surface), cracked, or ring dull instead of with a clear ringing tone when tapped; estimates accounting for breakage so enough are made.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Loads wet/damp tiles into a hot kiln or ramps heat fast through the early (water-smoking) stage, causing steam spalling.
Claims firing as hot/fast as possible is best, or omits the slow drying step entirely.
reference_facts: Roof tiles warp and crack mainly from uneven drying and shrinkage; uniform thickness and slow, even drying are the primary controls.
Earthenware roof tiles are fired to roughly 900-1000C; the ramp must be slow through ~100-200C to drive off water without steam explosions, with slow cooling to avoid thermal-shock cracks.
Tempering with ~10-20% sand or grog reduces drying shrinkage and warping.
A sound fired tile rings with a clear tone when tapped; a cracked or under-fired one sounds dull.
Tiles ~15-20 mm thick balance weather resistance against weight and drying time.

title: Vulcanise raw rubber boots with an unknown shed powder
scenario: Situation: Survivor tapped Hevea latex and moulded crude boots, but the raw rubber goes brittle in cold and softens/sticks in heat (untreated thermoplastic polyisoprene).

Resources: Three unlabelled jars from a workshop: (1) white powder that fizzes vigorously in vinegar; (2) bright-yellow crumbly powder smelling of eggs; (3) heavy white crystals tasting of salt. Plus a fire/oven for heat.
Constraint: Needs to identify which jar cures rubber and how to apply heat without ruining the boots.
prompt: I made crude boots from raw rubber tree sap, but they go brittle in the cold and nearly melted off my feet on a hot hike. I found three unlabelled jars in a shed: (1) a fine white powder that fizzes hard when I drop it in vinegar; (2) a bright-yellow crumbly powder that smells faintly of rotten eggs; (3) heavy white crystals that just taste like salt. Which jar, if any, can I use to treat the rubber so it stops softening in heat, and exactly how do I process the boots — what do I mix in, how much, at what temperature, for how long, and what do I watch for so I don't burn or destroy them?
rubric: Identifies jar (2), the yellow egg-smelling powder, as sulfur, and the active ingredient for curing the rubber.
Identifies jar (1) as a carbonate (e.g. sodium bicarbonate/washing soda — it fizzes in acid) and jar (3) as common salt, and states neither cures rubber.
Names the process as vulcanisation.
Specifies adding roughly 1-3 parts sulfur per 100 parts rubber by weight (a few percent) and kneading/milling it uniformly into the rubber before heating.
Explains the mechanism: heat makes sulfur form cross-links (sulfur bridges) between the polyisoprene chains.
States the cross-links stop the chains sliding past each other, so the rubber no longer softens/flows when hot and is less brittle when cold.
Specifies curing temperature of about 140-160 C (roughly 285-320 F) held for on the order of tens of minutes, not a brief flash of heat.
Warns that vulcanised rubber is a thermoset: form/shape the boots fully BEFORE curing because it cannot be remelted or reshaped afterward.
Warns the process gives off acrid sulfur fumes (sulfur dioxide / hydrogen sulfide) and must be done outdoors or with strong ventilation, away from open flame contacting the sulfur.
Gives a check that curing worked: a cured sample is springy and snaps back, does not get tacky/soft in sun and does not go stiff/cracking in cold, versus the gummy raw sample.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Selects the carbonate (jar 1) or the salt (jar 3) as the curing agent instead of sulfur.
Claims smoke-curing or simply drying the latex will stop the heat-softening (it preserves/coagulates but does not vulcanise).
Specifies a cure temperature high enough to char/burn the rubber (e.g. >250 C) or so low it never cross-links (e.g. ambient/<100 C).
reference_facts: Vulcanisation = heating natural rubber (polyisoprene) with sulfur to form sulfur cross-links between chains, converting thermoplastic rubber into an elastic thermoset.
Typical accelerator-free sulfur loading for cured rubber is a few percent by weight (~1-3 parts sulfur per 100 parts rubber for soft rubber; more sulfur gives harder rubber/ebonite).
Natural rubber cures at roughly 140-160 C; broad industrial range cited 140-200 C, held for minutes to tens of minutes.
Sulfur is yellow and smells of rotten eggs when impure/heated; a carbonate fizzes (releases CO2) in vinegar; table salt (NaCl) tastes salty and is inert here.
Vulcanised rubber is a thermoset: it cannot be remelted/reshaped, so it must be formed before curing.
Heating sulfur with rubber releases irritant sulfur oxide/hydrogen-sulfide fumes; needs ventilation.

title: Make crucible (wootz) ultra-high-carbon steel with a carbide pattern
scenario: A skilled smith wants to make a small ingot of patterned crucible (wootz) steel from clean bloomery iron/wrought iron plus a carbon source, melted fully liquid in a sealed clay crucible.
They have a forced-draught charcoal furnace capable of ~1500 C, lidded fireclay crucibles, charcoal/green wood as the carbon source, and the means to bury/insulate the crucible for slow cooling.
Target: ~1.3-1.6% carbon, a fully melted ingot, then a slow solidification and careful low-temperature forging to develop and preserve the dendritic carbide banding.
Etching with a mild acid will be used to reveal the pattern.
prompt: I want to make a small patterned crucible-steel (wootz) ingot. I have clean wrought/bloomery iron, charcoal and some green wood as a carbon source, lidded fireclay crucibles, a forced-draught charcoal furnace that reaches about 1500 C, and I can bury the hot crucible in ash/sand to cool it slowly. Walk me through it with numbers: what TARGET carbon percentage makes wootz (it's ultra-high carbon, well above ordinary steel) and why I must fully MELT it in a sealed crucible (unlike a bloomery) so the iron dissolves carbon evenly; why the crucible is lidded/sealed and what that does for the atmosphere and carbon pickup; the rough melt temperature; then the single most important step for the PATTERN — why the ingot must be cooled VERY slowly so carbide-forming elements segregate between dendrites into bands of cementite (Fe3C); and finally how to forge it WITHOUT destroying the pattern — the low/controlled forging temperature limit (you must NOT take it to a bright welding heat or full austenitising or you redissolve the carbides), thermal-cycling, and how to ETCH the finished blade to reveal the watered pattern. Give the failure modes that erase the pattern.
rubric: Specifies a target carbon content in the ultra-high-carbon range ~1.0-2.0% (ideally ~1.3-1.6%), distinctly above ordinary steel (~0.3-1.0% C).

States the iron must be fully MELTED to liquid in the crucible so carbon dissolves uniformly (melting point falls with carbon; the ~1.3-1.6% C melt is liquid around ~1400-1500 C), unlike the solid-state bloomery.
Explains the crucible is sealed/lidded (clay-luted lid) to control the atmosphere — limit oxidation/decarburisation and regulate carbon uptake from the charge — not left open.
Gives a furnace/melt temperature around ~1450-1500 C sustained long enough to fully liquefy and homogenise the charge.
States the pattern is bands/clusters of cementite (iron carbide, Fe3C); carbide-forming trace elements (e.g. V, Cr, Mn) and carbon segregate between dendrites during freezing.
Specifies VERY SLOW cooling/solidification (bury/insulate the crucible) so this interdendritic segregation forms — fast cooling destroys the segregation and the pattern potential.

Warns the ingot must be forged at LOW/controlled temperature (roughly dull/medium red, well below welding heat and below full austenitising — a working range commonly cited ~650-850 C), because overheating redissolves the carbides and erases the pattern.
Describes repeated low-temperature forging/thermal cycling to spheroidise and align the carbides into the watered/banded pattern (not a single high-heat draw-out).
Specifies revealing the pattern by polishing then ETCHING with a mild acid (e.g. dilute ferric chloride, dilute nitric/acetic, or citrus/vinegar) which differentially attacks matrix vs carbide bands.

Names the pattern-killing failure modes: overheating to welding/austenitising heat redissolving carbides, and quenching/fast-cooling the ingot — both erase the watered pattern even if the steel is hard.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends fast-cooling/quenching the cast ingot to set the pattern (this destroys the interdendritic carbide segregation).
Recommends forging the ingot at bright welding/full-austenitising heat as the way to develop the pattern (this redissolves the carbides and erases it).
reference_facts: Wootz/crucible steel is ultra-high carbon, typically ~1.0-2.0% C (commonly ~1.3-1.6%), well above plain carbon steel.

Wootz is made by fully melting iron + carbon source in a sealed (clay-luted lid) crucible so carbon dissolves uniformly; the high-carbon melt liquefies around ~1400-1500 C, unlike solid-state bloomery iron.
The watered pattern is bands/clusters of cementite (Fe3C); carbide-forming trace elements (notably vanadium, also Cr/Mn/Mo) and carbon segregate interdendritically during slow solidification.
Very slow cooling/solidification is required to preserve interdendritic segregation; fast cooling destroys it and the pattern cannot form.

Forging must stay at low/controlled temperature (commonly ~650-850 C); overheating to welding or full austenitising heat redissolves carbides and erases the pattern. The pattern is revealed by polishing and etching with a mild acid (e.g. dilute ferric chloride or vinegar/citrus).

title: Melt a soda-lime glass batch and anneal it to stop shattering
scenario: A workshop wants to make crude soda-lime glass (for glazing, bottles, lab vessels) from raw materials: clean quartz sand, soda ash (sodium carbonate) from a wood-ash/lime process or trade, and slaked-lime/limestone.
They have a fuel-fired furnace able to reach ~1450-1500 C and clay crucibles, plus a separate insulated chamber/lehr that can hold ~500 C.
Past attempts cracked on cooling or came out cloudy/crystalline.
No instruments beyond colour judgement and a way to hold the lehr warm.
prompt: I want to make crude soda-lime glass from scratch. I have clean quartz sand, soda ash (sodium carbonate), and limestone/slaked lime, a furnace that can reach about 1450-1500 C, clay crucibles, and a separate insulated box that can hold around 500 C. My past batches either cracked apart while cooling or came out cloudy and full of crystals. Give me: the batch recipe by weight as approximate oxide percentages (silica, soda, lime) and why the soda is there (to drop silica's ~1700 C melting point) and why I must NOT just leave it as pure silica+soda (water-soluble water-glass) — i.e. why lime is essential; the melt temperature and roughly how long to soak so it 'fines' and the bubbles clear; why overshooting the lime causes devitrification (crystallisation/cloudiness) and the rough lime ceiling; and CRITICALLY the annealing step — what the annealing point and strain point are roughly, why fast cooling locks in stress that shatters the glass, and how to use my ~500 C box to anneal and slow-cool. Give numbers and the failure modes for both the cracking and the cloudiness.
rubric: Gives a soda-lime batch as approximate oxide percentages around 70-75% SiO2, 13-17% Na2O, 5-12% CaO (e.g. ~74/16/10), within accepted ranges.
Explains soda (Na2O from sodium carbonate) is a flux that lowers silica's melting point (pure SiO2 ~1700 C) to a workable ~1400-1500 C.
States that silica + soda alone yields water-soluble sodium silicate (water glass); lime (CaO) is the stabiliser that makes the glass water-insoluble/durable — so lime is essential, not optional.
Gives a melt temperature around 1450-1500 C and a soak/hold so the melt 'fines' (bubbles/seeds rise out and the batch homogenises) before forming.

Warns that excess lime (roughly CaO above ~12%) promotes devitrification — crystallisation that makes the glass cloudy/weak — and ties the cloudy-batch failure to over-liming or too-low/too-slow melt.

States that glass has no sharp melting point; identifies the annealing point (~510-560 C for soda-lime) where stress relaxes in minutes, and the strain point (~470-510 C) below which it is effectively rigid.
Explains that fast/uneven cooling freezes in permanent internal (residual) stress that makes the glass crack spontaneously or shatter — this is the cause of the cracking failure.
Directs holding the formed glass in the ~500 C lehr near the annealing point (soak to equalise temperature), then cooling SLOWLY through the strain point before allowing it to reach room temperature.
Specifies the slow cool must be gradual (avoid drafts/quenching) and the thicker the piece the slower the cool, to prevent a steep temperature gradient.

Names a verification: a properly annealed piece shows little/no stress birefringence under crossed polarised light (or simply survives normal handling/thermal change), whereas badly annealed glass cracks from minor stress.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Omits lime/stabiliser and presents pure silica-soda glass as durable usable glass (it would be water-soluble water glass).
Recommends quenching or fast-cooling the finished glass to room temperature with no anneal/slow-cool step (guaranteeing residual-stress fracture).
reference_facts: Typical soda-lime glass: ~70-75% SiO2, ~13-17% Na2O, ~5-12% CaO by weight (commonly ~74 SiO2 / 16 Na2O / 10 CaO).

Soda (Na2O) is a flux lowering silica's melting point from ~1700 C to a workable ~1400-1500 C; lime (CaO) stabilises the glass against water attack — silica+soda alone is soluble sodium silicate (water glass).
Excess CaO (above ~12%) promotes devitrification (crystallisation), giving cloudy/weakened glass.
Soda-lime glass anneals near ~510-560 C (annealing point ~548 C) and is effectively rigid below the strain point ~470-510 C (~511 C); it has no sharp melting point.
Annealing relieves residual stress: hold near the annealing point then cool slowly through the strain point; rapid/uneven cooling locks in stress that causes spontaneous cracking/shattering.

title: Run a bloomery smelt: charge ratio, airflow, slag, bloom
scenario: A small settlement has a clay-and-stone shaft bloomery ~30 cm internal diameter, one tuyere, and a foot-powered double-bag bellows.
They have ~40 kg of roasted, crushed bog/rock iron ore (mostly iron oxide) sieved to roughly pea size, and abundant hardwood charcoal broken to walnut size.
Goal: produce a forgeable iron bloom, not a pool of cast iron, and consolidate it into a usable billet.
No thermocouple; the smith judges by flame colour, slag, and feel.
prompt: I have a clay-and-stone shaft furnace about 30 cm wide inside with a single tuyere and a foot-bellows. I've roasted and crushed about 40 kg of iron-oxide ore to pea size and have plenty of hardwood charcoal broken to walnut size. I want to smelt a forgeable iron BLOOM, not accidentally make brittle cast iron. Walk me through the whole smelt: what charcoal-to-ore charge ratio (by volume) to feed and why a roughly 2:1 to 3:1 charcoal:ore ratio keeps the chemistry reducing; roughly what bed/hearth temperature I need for the slag to run and what the reaction is that actually reduces the ore to metal (carbon monoxide, not melting the iron); how to set the tuyere height and angle and how to read the air blast; what fayalite slag is, why I WANT it to form and tap it, and what tapping/colour tells me it's working; how to keep the bloom from absorbing too much carbon and turning to unforgeable cast iron; and finally how to extract, consolidate and forge the spongy bloom (welding heat, working out the slag). Give me numbers and the failure modes I should watch for.
rubric: Specifies a charcoal:ore charge of roughly 2:1 to 3:1 by volume (or a comparable slight charcoal excess), maintained throughout the smelt to keep a reducing (CO-rich) atmosphere.
States that solid-state reduction is driven by carbon monoxide (CO) reducing iron oxide to metallic iron WITHOUT melting the iron (iron melts ~1538 C, far above furnace temp).
Gives a hearth/bottom temperature target around 1200 C, the point at which fayalite slag becomes fluid and separates from the metal.

Explains fayalite is an iron-silicate slag (2FeO·SiO2) that melts near 1150-1200 C, and that its forming/running carries away gangue and protects the bloom — so it should be tapped or let run, not avoided.

Warns that too high a charcoal:ore ratio / too long in a strongly carburising zone lets the iron absorb carbon and become brittle unforgeable cast iron (high-carbon, melts lower ~1150 C and drips), and that this is the cast-iron failure to avoid.
Directs preheating/drying the furnace and charging in alternating layers of charcoal then ore, topping up charcoal to keep the stack full and the burn reducing.

Specifies setting the tuyere a fixed height above the hearth bottom (roughly 15-25 cm) angled slightly downward, and keeping a steady continuous blast so the hottest reducing zone sits at/below the tuyere where the bloom builds.
States the bloom is a spongy, porous mass of iron mixed with slag that forms at/below the tuyere and must be pulled out hot (not poured), since it never fully melts.
Specifies consolidating the bloom by repeated heating to a welding/forging heat (bright yellow-white, ~1200-1300 C) and hammering to expel entrapped slag and weld the sponge into solid billet.

Names a concrete read of success/failure: free-running glassy slag and a coherent magnetic bloom = good; cold short/crumbly bloom, no slag running, or a melted dripping high-carbon mass = wrong, and ties each to a fix (more air/temperature, adjust ratio).
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Claims the bloomery melts the iron into liquid that is poured like cast iron (the bloom forms solid-state and is never poured).
Recommends a strongly oxidising (excess-air, fuel-lean) burn, which would re-oxidise the iron and prevent reduction.
reference_facts: Bloomery reduction is solid-state: CO from incomplete charcoal combustion reduces iron oxides to metallic iron below iron's melting point (Fe melts ~1538 C); the bloom is never molten.
Typical charcoal:ore charge is roughly 2:1 to 3:1 by volume; a charcoal excess maintains a CO-rich reducing atmosphere.
Fayalite (2FeO·SiO2, iron-silicate slag) melts ~1150-1205 C; a hearth temperature near 1200 C is needed for slag to run and separate from the bloom.
Excess carburisation produces high-carbon cast iron, which is brittle/unforgeable and melts lower (~1150-1300 C), dripping instead of forming a workable bloom.
The bloom is a porous sponge of iron + entrapped slag that must be drawn out hot and consolidated by forge-welding (bright yellow-white heat ~1200-1300 C) and hammering to expel slag.
